sentences.text: You were talking about a baby being born--
Yeah.
It's-- it was very hard for the-- for the mother, what was-- it was a shame.
But the baby is now-- [LAUGHS] she, she is now in, in New York, you know.
She got-- she got married.
She has already children.
And we, we are going-- I went to the wedding.
It was very hard.
Not only she-- one, one lady was with a-- with a child, but she escaped with the child.
The child was probably about three or four years, you know.
She was a doctor.
And then she has a-- had a very hard time with that child.
She has to cover up.
She has to put pillows on here, that-- but we did the best we can.
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not specifically.
Everybody, you know, care about his family-- everybody.
But we worked together.
We work together.
We, we tried to help each other.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
[CRYING] [SOBBING] Nobody can imagine.
[CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But when the Russian cames-- came, we decided-- we decided-- we were very happy, you know.
Mosheh ?] come.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So then we get all together.
We knew already that the camp there in Malinsk there.
So we got everybody together.
So no-- now we get-- we getting out of the woods.
But I remember how afraid we were go out, you know.
We are afraid for everybody.
We-- we went to the railroad, you know.
The Germans were not there already because-- so-- and we were-- we think everybody is going to shoot us.
We were afraid for people.
No-- when we came-- it was the Russians, they did their job.

Where they-- but to say that they are special, they going to-- the-- OK, they, they came because they-- there's-- were a lot of young, Jewish guys they took right away in the-- they want to go in the army.
They sent them together with the Ukrainian and with the-- they're called [NON-ENGLISH].
They were killers.
And on the way, they killed them, this, you know, the-- these Ukrainians, you know.
They-- it was not organized.
They used to go, and they used to sleep in the-- you know, in the army, you know.
They used to-- they took them to the army.
But until they came to the army, we had that-- a lot of the Jewish guys got killed.
It's not like they told, oh, you're Jewish.
We're not going to-- you survived already.
No, that's-- But we were happy they, they liberated us.
There's no question about it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we have nowhere to go, you know.
No family-- we are four brothers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I, in [NON-ENGLISH], decided to go into army.
It was a front.
It was-- but what can be worse from there?
We decided to go in the army.
So the other two brothers said, we are going-- we want to-- how do you call, to, to, to show who were the killers.
You know, we want to take a little bit revenge, you know, to show who were the killers because we knew.
And they did it, you know.
A lot, a lot of the-- Shimon Bogart and-- so they didn't go.
They want-- the-- it was the, the Russian, what it was-- they start to show who was with the Nazis, who were, you know.
But meanwhile-- but they have to go.
They were still in the woods, the-- all the [NON-ENGLISH], and all the-- and to look for them again.
A lot of them got killed.
But they survived with us.
That Shimon Bogart was very brilliant.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He got killed among others.
No-- my brother Yossel, he, he survived.
He worked until it was problem in 1943, matter of fact.
I had a letter in the army that he got killed.
He got killed.
He, he want to go to Korost.
He want to see what's going on, I mean, there.
So on the way, he went through-- that was a-- from the Russians.
It was a truck, you know.
He went through them.
And it was-- it was there from the, the Ukrainian.
And they used to make, like, in the woods-- how do you call it?
They used to-- you desert, they, they used to shoot.
So they-- he went with the Russian soldiers.
And they start-- the Ukrainian from they woods start to shoot toward them.
Yossel got killed.
This was after-- [CRYING] [SOBBING] after all-- what he get through.
Just Yossel got-- Aaron got killed.
I don't know.
They send him in the army.
I don't know exactly what happened.
But he got killed.
So we went toward the danger.
I and my brother Monia, we went on the front.
I was wounded two times on the front.
I was on the first line.
They sent us to-- in Kharkov.
I, Yossel, and another-- a few more guys what we were together.
So they keep us for six months in Kharkov.
First of all, they took us.
And, and they give us their uniform.
And they-- we went to take a shower.
Can, can you imagine how we felt?
It felt like you're born after that-- after the-- so we felt already-- [LAUGHS] I remember that.
And so-- we went-- we, we were-- six months we were in the-- in the woods, yeah, I mean in the-- in the army, you know.
They prepare us, you know, how to go out on the front.
So they sent me on the Warsaw, you know, Germany-- street.
And Monia, they sent on the Baltimore-- another front.
So I, I had a bad time there.
I was on the first-- on the first line.
And I was-- wondered why I was there.
And I have-- third time, I had the accident with the motorcycle what this is-- this is-- was.
But I was wounded on the front twice.
But thank God, after all that, I survived.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In 19-- it's a lot to talk about the army.
But what, what, what is there is, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But in 1946, it came time to demobilize it.
It's just-- from the army.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so then I questioned where to go.
I have nobody.
So-- but somewhere, after the war, I contact my brother.
He, he was alive, too.
And we, we decided that we're going to meet each other in Kostopol.
In Kostopol, we had a friend, what she-- that was from Korost, too.
She survived.
They are survived.
The three sisters survived and a brother.
They were in partisans with that [?
Rafael, ?] what I had said.
He, he want to contact us with-- and they survived.
And they were in Kostopol.
So we came to them.
We were awhile with them.
And then we decided we have to-- we have to start a new life.
So we went to-- we, we met a friend, a good friend, what we knew from before.
And he said, if you want to get married or what, in Korosten, there is Jewish girls.
So we went to, to Korosten, my brother and I.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my brother met my wife's sister.
He got married.
And I got married with her.
So we took two brother, two sisters.
How long were you in Korosten?
In Korosten, we were-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we-- I came '46, '47.
I'd say probably about a year and a half.
You know, we were in Korosten, we went to-- used to travel to, to Lwow, you know.
Later, we, we got married.
We decided to move to Lwow.
And we live in Lwow together.
We got married in 1949.
So since then, we were in Lwow.
My brother was in Lwow, too.
So we were in Lwow.
We were in Lwow until we try-- we tried to-- we want-- our goal is to go to Israel, you know.
And we couldn't-- it was a time that the Jewish people could leave Russia, you know, in the beginning.
But when we came from the army, it was already too late.
And so it's closed.
It was closed.
We were late for a month or two.
All the echelons were closed.
And we couldn't.
So we try all kinds of things.
We couldn't-- we couldn't do it.
So we wait.
Until 1957, we were in Lwow.
Yeah, I work in Lwow.
My wife worked.
And in 1957, there were opportunity because Gomulka-- Khrushchev make a deal with Gomulka that all the Polish citizens can return back to Poland, you know.
My goal was not to go to Poland.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [CRYING] But we went to Poland.
And we were in Poland three years.
And we went to Israel.
To Korost?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I-- when I came from, from the army, I want to go to Korost.
And I met one-- a friend, you know, a survivor, too.
He said, let's go to Korost.
I want to see.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We went to Korost, you know.
We walked to Korost, it-- 8 kilometer.
So everybody-- you know, it was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] heartbreaking.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Somebody told me that, that one guy took our house.
And he moved to-- so he didn't do it.
One policeman, he took our house from the place.
Not the original house-- we had another house, what it was there, like a business.
So he took the other house.
And he moved the policeman to him.
And later, when the Russians came, they send the policeman to Siberia.
And that man-- he was a poor man-- he took his house.
He was living in that house.
So I came to him.
And I said, do you know whose house this is?
He said, yeah, I know.
But I didn't-- I didn't take it, you know, the other guy-- the policeman.
And now I'm living here in-- I said, but you know it's my house.
At that, he said, you know what?
I'll-- I have a cow.
I'll sell the cow.
I said, I didn't go.
But it was-- this what I have the-- you know, you saw there, that he gave me-- that this is my house.
And I never went back there.
And this was in-- and we were so afraid.
I thought we were lucky that we came back, you know, after that.
Since then, it was still a dangerous time.
Since then, we never went there.
Yeah.
Oh, to-- oh, Poland, we left in 1960.
We-- I came in 1957.
It was a time, you know, it was a time when the same thing, like-- my brother filed the papers in Russia at the same time with I to go.
He is a Polish citizen, too.
So he filed the papers.
And they go to Poland.
And in two weeks, you could go to Israel, you know.
It was a transit.
But when we came, it, it was some reason they didn't give me-- so, yeah, Russia, they didn't let me go so fast.
They keep me there for few-- four months.
And they, they find all kinds excuses that I'm not-- my papers are not good.
Something that maybe I'm not from-- not a Polish citizen.
They didn't-- but finally, they let us out.
And when I came, it was already closed.
They-- you couldn't go to, to Israel anymore, you know.
It was a-- but after three years, they opened again the-- in Poland, you know, I worked like a furrier, you know.
In Wroclaw-- it was Breslau, Breslau.
Breslau, B-R-E-S-L-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] V, I don't know-- Breslau, Breslau.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Used to be-- in Polish is Wroclaw.
They used to call Wroclaw.
I work in a-- in the beginning, I, I start to learn to work by meat, and then I think it was like a-- later, I work in a [NON-ENGLISH], you know, like a delicatessen in Israel.
I live in a-- in the beginning-- in the beginning, we live [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, it was-- [NON-ENGLISH] is like something, just temporary houses, you know, in, in Bat Yam.
And later, I got [NON-ENGLISH].
So I was very happy in Israel.
I mean, we-- my brother was there.
But for some reason, my wife has her two aunts.
And they start to-- they didn't have nobody come over, kind of would like to have somebody.
And I didn't want to go.
It-- and they send-- they were-- they were poor people.
They didn't have-- they didn't have children.
And they were very poor.
They live in a-- in a black neighborhood, you know, [NON-ENGLISH].
So-- but they want us to come.
And we didn't.
So they wrote a letter.
They said, if you're not going to take that opportunity, somebody-- a friend-- send affidavit.
Well, he's-- he was a rich man.
They couldn't-- she said, we couldn't do it.
That, that man did me a big favor.
If you're not-- so we didn't want to go.
But people-- oh, you have the opportunity to go to America.
You don't want to go?
And the last minute, we did everything.
We came to America.
Did you have a family of your own by then?
Oh, yeah, my-- the children were already-- and my wife here.
We got married in, in Russia, you know.
In Russia.
What you mean?
What commanders you mean?
Every--
Oh, everybody got killed.
These, these were the 500 wagons that came.
When they brought in these, everybody-- this was the final.
They took everybody to under Kostopol, [NON-ENGLISH].
There is, you know, woods where they-- and everybody got killed at this.
You know, the people-- the people from Korost, the Christians, they used to-- from there-- was a lot of wagons from there.
And they came, and they told us.
They told us exactly what happened.
They saw the families.
And they, they, they told us the stories and who escaped.
You know, they used to-- they used to catch people.
And they used to take him to the cemetery.
And they used to shoot him there.
One, one Christian-- we came to, to ask for food-- nighttime.
So he said, you know, I was yesterday in Stepan.
It was already when nobody was there.
So I, I went to the-- on the cemetery.
And I saw-- and he called the name who this is.
They killing that baba.
She was still alive, asking, please, please, kill me.
Please, kill me, you know.
They, they let him-- but he said, I, I didn't have a heart to do it.
Only two brothers.
Yeah.
We are very close.
He is coming here.
And we are going there.
We are very close.
My brother has now a family.
He has a-- has, has a son and a-- a son and a daughter and grandchildren, six, six grandchildren.
And we are very close.
We are going to them and have a-- the best vacation.
To Lwow-- to, to Philadelphia.
Our--
Our aunts were in Philadelphia.
But they were in a neighborhood-- they were in a neighborhood that there is-- were-- they only two-- it was Strawberry Mansion.
I don't know if you're familiar with it-- if you know it.
It was Strawberry Mansion, a very good neighborhood in a-- one time.
But when we came, there was no Jewish people anymore, but only two, two, two sisters.
They were in a house.
They couldn't move because they didn't have the strength already.
So we, we lived with them for, for 10 months.
They were very happy to have us.
We-- they were very happy to have us.
So our children are supposed to take-- we couldn't send the children in that school because they were only two, two white children.
We sent the children in McCall School-- you know, the McCall School there for immigrants.
But they have to take two buses, you know, there.
But in a short time when I start to work, we, we, we moved to Wynnefield.
We rent a apartment.
And we rent apartment for them.
[CRYING] [SOBBING] They want to be near us.
They sold their house for $2,800.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they-- we lived to-- in Wynnefield, they lived nearby.
They were very old ladies.
And later, we moved in Northeast.
We bought a house in Northeast.
So they moved with us.
They died already.
[CRYING] Hardest question.
[CRYING] I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Things what I went through, I don't know how I survived.
[CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBBING] After everything, the front-- it's miracle.
[CRYING] All the time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBBING] I have bad dreams.
The first year-- I-- few years-- many years, I couldn't-- why?
For what?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Children-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for what?
Would [SOB] [CRYING] How I survived-- I-- it's a miracle.
I can't say I'm smart or what.
It's just a miracle.
It's probably got-- to want somebody to tell the story.
I couldn't.
I couldn't.
I couldn't tell.
[SOBBING] I should start to talk.
I should start.
Only one time-- I was at my daughter's house.
And she said, Dad, try.
Maybe, maybe you'll get--
So one time, you know, I, I, I told them a lot.
I mean, but it came to a point I couldn't talk anymore.
Why did you decide you want to tell your story now?
I always want people to know.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I want my children to and my grandchildren, you know-- people has to know this story.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Because it was no reason to do something like that to people what they didn't do anything wrong.
I, I try my best for my children.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CRYING] I don't know how to answer this question.
I tried the best to do the best for them.
I don't want them to, to go through [SOBBING] what we went through.
